coredata.dc:title: Optimizing humic acid breakdown and methane production via semiconductor-assisted photo-anaerobic digestion
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Qiang
Zhang, Haoyu
Chen, Shuo
Li, Yeqing
Mazarji, Mahmoud
Feng, Lu
Pan, Junting
Zhou, Hongjun
Xu, Chunming
coredata.dc:description: Humic acids (HAs), whether naturally present in anaerobic digestion (AD) substrates or HAs newly formed during fermentation, can become inhibitory to methanogenesis as their concentrations reach certain levels. This study delved extensively into the mechanism underlying the alleviation of HAs inhibition in photo-AD by N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs). These NCQDs were efficiently synthesized from straw using an environmentally friendly pretreatment method. Our proposed method harnessed the combined effect of light exposure and NCQDs, resulting in synergistic enhancement of the cumulative CH4 yield within the HA-inhibited AD system, achieving a remarkable yield of 293.7 ± 17.7 mL/g VS. In-depth analyses were conducted on the remaining dissolved organic matter (DOM) within digesters using 3D-EEM and ESI FT-ICR MS. Simultaneously, the remaining HAs were extracted and subjected to FT-IR analysis. The findings revealed that NCQDs effectively degraded humic acid-like components in DOM into smaller, more manageable micromolecules characterized by lower carbon numbers and reduced double bond equivalent. Additionally, under the influence of light, NCQDs promoted the degradation of aromatic components within HAs. These resulting micromolecules were made readily available for utilization by microorganisms, further contributing to methanogenesis. Furthermore, photoelectrochemical analysis and specific gene qPCR analysis revealed that the photoelectrons from NCQDs and HAs were received and transferred by Ech (electron acceptors) for methanogenesis. Remarkably, this methanogenesis pathway, akin to photosynthesis, played a pivotal and transformative role in the photo-AD system. This work comprehensively revealed the remarkable potential mechanism of semiconductors within the photo-AD system, offering profound insights that can catalyze the development of innovative AD reactors and semiconductor accelerators.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Humic acids
N-carbon quantum dots
ESI FT-ICR MS
Methanogenesis

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Green and Sustainable Science and Technology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Microbial community structure and functional genes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22278435), the Earmarked Fund for CARS (No. CARS-06-14.5-A31), the Strategic Cooperation Technology Projects of CNPC and CUPB (No. ZLZX2020-04), Carbon Neutrality Research Institute Fund (No. 20220202\uFF0CNo. 20230102).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Green method employed for synthesizing straw-based NCQDs (Schematic diagram 1). TEM images (a ∼ c) and High-resolution TEM of straw-based NCQDs (e). Reverse FFT micrographs and patterns of interplanar spacing (f ∼ h). The FT-IR spectrum (i) and XPS spectrum (j ∼ l) of straw-based NCQDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The variation of cumulative CH4 yield (a) and daily CH4 yield (b) with the passage of time. EPR signals of •OH detected over HAs and NCQDs in aqueous (c).
The remaining sCOD concentration in various batch AD systems (a); EEM spectra of DOM in these different AD systems (b ∼ f).
The Van Krevelen diagram of DOM in water solution (a) and as well as in different AD systems containing HAs (b ∼ d). The various colored wireframes represent different sub-class components.

Plots of DBE against C number for CHO in different systems (a). Plot of DBE-O6 against C number plot for CHO in in different systems (b ∼ e). Schematic diagram illustrating how NCQDs promote the photodegradation of HAs (f).

The CV curves (a) and photocurrent (b) of HAs and NCQDs. UV–Vis diffuse reflectance absorption spectra of HAs (c). Photocurrent (d) and ESI (e) of remaining solid substrates in different digesters. Schematic representation of photo-driven methanogenesis at the biotic-abiotic interface (f).

Venn diagram illustrating the distribution of bacterial (a) and archaea (b) at OTU level, along with the relative abundance of bacteria (c) and archaea (d) at Class level. The concentration of ATP and coenzyme F420 (e), and the proportion of mean gene copynumbers of specific genes encoding functional proteins in microbial communities from different experimental groups (f).
The mechanism of NCQDs in HAs-inhibited photo-AD systems highlighting the promotion of NCQDs, which accelerate degradation and generate photoelectrons under light conditions.

head.ce:title: Optimizing humic acid breakdown and methane production via semiconductor-assisted photo-anaerobic digestion

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing
Beijing Key Laboratory of Biogas Upgrading Utilization, College of New Energy and Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Humic acids (HAs), whether naturally present in anaerobic digestion (AD) substrates or HAs newly formed during fermentation, can become inhibitory to methanogenesis as their concentrations reach certain levels. This study delved extensively into the mechanism underlying the alleviation of HAs inhibition in photo-AD by N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs). These NCQDs were efficiently synthesized from straw using an environmentally friendly pretreatment method. Our proposed method harnessed the combined effect of light exposure and NCQDs, resulting in synergistic enhancement of the cumulative CH4 yield within the HA-inhibited AD system, achieving a remarkable yield of 293.7 ± 17.7 mL/g VS. In-depth analyses were conducted on the remaining dissolved organic matter (DOM) within digesters using 3D-EEM and ESI FT-ICR MS. Simultaneously, the remaining HAs were extracted and subjected to FT-IR analysis. The findings revealed that NCQDs effectively degraded humic acid-like components in DOM into smaller, more manageable micromolecules characterized by lower carbon numbers and reduced double bond equivalent. Additionally, under the influence of light, NCQDs promoted the degradation of aromatic components within HAs. These resulting micromolecules were made readily available for utilization by microorganisms, further contributing to methanogenesis. Furthermore, photoelectrochemical analysis and specific gene qPCR analysis revealed that the photoelectrons from NCQDs and HAs were received and transferred by Ech (electron acceptors) for methanogenesis. Remarkably, this methanogenesis pathway, akin to photosynthesis, played a pivotal and transformative role in the photo-AD system. This work comprehensively revealed the remarkable potential mechanism of semiconductors within the photo-AD system, offering profound insights that can catalyze the development of innovative AD reactors and semiconductor accelerators.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Green, sustainable development is the theme of the world today. Green treatment and disposal are essential pathways towards achieving sustainable development in the face of large quantities of organic waste. During anaerobic digestion (AD), organic matter is converted into biogas and digestate through hydrolysis, acidogenesis, and methanogenesis [1]. Biogas, which is primarily composed of ∼ 70 % CH4, can serve as an alternative energy source (the calorific value of biogas is 17.9 ∼ 29.9 MJ/Nm3 under standard conditions) to fossil fuels for reducing carbon emissions [2]. However, the conversion efficiency of AD systems relies on the activity of microorganisms. The metabolism rate of these microorganisms is often inhibited by unsuitable environments, such as high concentrations of ammonia nitrogen, volatile fatty acids (VFAs), antibiotics, heavy metal ions, and humic acids (HAs) [3–6].

In recent years, the impact of HAs on AD has gained widespread attention. Multiple researches have shown that HAs primarily inhibit AD during the hydrolysis and methanogenesis stages [7]. In both stages, HAs inhibited microbial activity by reducing enzyme activity through binding with proteases, α-glucosidases, and coenzyme F420 [8]. This inhibition significantly affects the conversion efficiency. In some studies, the HAs can inhibit up to 74.3 % of the conversion efficiency (cumulative CH4 yield) in semi-continuous AD [9]. Currently, the approaches to alleviate the inhibition of HAs include extraction, adding excess hydrolytic enzymes, and adding appropriate amounts of metal ions [7,10,11]. These methods are limited due to poor operability, high costs, and negative impacts on microorganisms. Our previous research found that N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) will bind to HAs in situ, thereby shielding the inhibition [12]. This method is simple to operate and has no adverse effects on microorganisms. However, compared to the extraction of HAs and the addition of hydrolytic enzymes, NCQDs only shield the inhibition of HAs but cannot eliminate its [7].

Photocatalytic degradation is a standard method for removing complex organic compounds [13]. NCQDs is a type of photocatalyst commonly used to enhance catalytic performance when loaded onto other catalyst [14]. And HAs also possess potential photocatalytic properties. Previous research has found that organic compounds can act as photoelectrochemical materials in aquatic environments [15,16]. Furthermore, current research indicated that light was benefited AD, as it promoted microbial aggregation and enhanced electron transfer levels [17]. Therefore, providing light to AD systems containing NCQDs and HAs holds dual potential effects for increasing methane yield and removing HAs.

In this study, NCQDs were synthesized from straw using the hydrothermal method, aligning with sustainability principles and green chemistry. The study aimed to explore the effects of NCQDs on relieving the inhibition of HAs, explicitly focusing on unveiling the degradative effects of NCQDs. The study implemented a multifaceted analytical strategy encompassing three-dimensional fluorescence technology to elucidate the impact of NCQDs on the substrate. Furthermore, the investigation delved into the photoelectrochemical properties of substrates and microbial community structure and enzyme abundance, shedding light on the intricate mechanisms involving light-responsive materials in the photo-AD system. This study provides a new method for further dealing with HAs shielding and comprehensively reveals the mechanism of photo-responsive materials in the photo-AD system.

A pretreatment process was conducted to prepare the corn straw for the synthesis of NCQDs [18–20]. Initially, the straw was crushed and passed through a 40-mesh sieve. Subsequently, 0.5 g of straw powder was immersed in a solution containing 5.0 mL of acetic acid and 5.0 mL of H2O2. This mixture was stirred at room temperature (25 ± 5 °C) for 5 days. After that, an additional 5.0 mL H2O2 was introduced, and the solution was stirred for an additional 5 days. After this 10-day period, the mixture underwent centrifugation and was then subjected to heating at 60 °C to eliminate any residual H2O2 and acetic acid, resulting in the formation of a white powdered sample.

Subsequently, NCQDs were synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method [21]. The pretreated straws were dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water. The mixture was then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor and heated at 190 °C for 500 min. After the reactor cooled to room temperature, the resulting brown solution was centrifuged and filtered. The residue obtained after drying was referred to as NCQDs.

In order to improve the light transmittance, the reactor used in this experiment was borosilicate serum bottle, which light transmittance was greater than 90 %. The photobioreactor was assembled by wrapping three loops of a full spectrum LED light strip (with an intensity of 250 W/m2) around a 100 mL borosilicate glass serum bottle with a working volume of 80 mL ( Fig. S1 ) [22]. Besides, a dark bioreactor was constructed by wrapping it in tin foil. The fermentation substrate used was glucose at a concentration of 5.0 g/L. The inoculum was obtained from a large-scale anaerobic digester and was domesticated prior to inoculation, maintaining the inoculum concertation at 10 g/L at the beginning of the experiment. Five different experiments were set up, including dark-AD (R), dark-AD with HAs (R(HA)), photo-AD (RL), photo-AD with HAs (R(HA)L), photo-AD with HAs, and NCQDs (R(HA)L NCQD). These experiments involved the addition of 1.0 g/L commercial HAs (CAS: 1415–93-6) and 0.5 g/L of NCQDs to the respective systems. The methane yield of different experimental groups was evaluated using Biochemical Methane Potential apparatus (BMP) consisting of fermentation unit, tail gas absorption unit, and gas flow recording unit. The fermentation unit comprised 15 reactors equipped with a heating magnetic stirring panel and a temperature control feedback probe with stirring at 120 rpm and a temperature of 37 °C. The reactors received 12 h of light per day. The absorption unit included bottles with NaOH solution (15 ∼ 50 g/L), with one bottle connected to each reactor. The absorption unit consists of absorption bottles containing NaOH solution (15 ∼ 50 g/L), with one absorption bottle connected to each reactor. Before startup, N2 was continuously introduced for 2 ∼ 3 min.

The morphology and structure of straw-based NCQDs were investigated through a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-F200, Japan) and a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEOL JEM-F200, Japan). Surface functional groups of the sample were identified using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20, USA) to detect the surface functional groups of the sample. Elemental composition and chemical state analysis of NCQDs were conducted using X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi, USA) was used to detect the elemental composition and chemical state of NCQDs. Additionally, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy was utilized to analyze radicals, which was carried out using the A300 (Bruker, USA).

Obtain the solution from the anaerobic digester and analyze the soluble chemical oxygen demand (sCOD) using standard methods (APHA). The components and content of organic matter in the solution were detected by three-dimensional Excitation-Emission-Matrix (3D-EEM) spectra and flourier transform ion cyclotron resonance mass spectrometry (ESI FT-ICR MS) (Text S1). After AD, extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) were extracted from each reactor, including loosely bound EPS (LB-EPS) and tightly bound EPS (TB-EPS) [17]. The protocol for EPS extraction is as Text S2. The total organic carbon (TOC) of LB-EPS and TB-EPS was determined using a TOC analyzer (Analytik Jena, multi-N/C 2100 s, Germany). Additionally, the total protein content of EPS was quantified using the Lowry method [23], and the polysaccharides content was determined using the alkaline-acid precipitation method (Text S3) [24].

According to our previous study, substrates were subjected to high-throughput 16S rRNA analysis to detect the microbial community structure [25]. ATP test methods were based on the assay kit purchased by Wuhan Biowefind Biotechnology Co., Ltd and used according to manufacturer’s instructions. Coenzyme F420 was detected using spectrophotometer method (Text S4).

The analysis of EchA, VhtA, and HdrD content was conducted using qPCR services provided by Shanghai Meiji Biotechnology Co., Ltd. The RNA-extracted primers used during this detection process are detailed in Table S2 . The gene copynumber was determined based on the standard curve ( Table S3 ) and Ct (cyclic threshold), as illustrated in Eq. (I) and (II).
Ct = k · logCopynumber + b (I)

C o p y n u m b e r
=
10
C t - b
k
(II)
Where k and b are slope and Y-intercept, respectively.

Before electrochemical analysis, the sample was evenly coated on one side of an indium tin oxide (ITO) conductive glass with dimensions of 10 mm × 20 mm. After drying at room temperature (25 °C), an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, Chenhua Co., Shanghai, China) was used for all analyses, with an Ag/AgCl electrode and a platinum foil electrode serving as the reference electrode and the counter electrode, respectively.

For the I-t curve analysis, a 50 W LED lamp was used as the light source, alternating between on and off states at an interval of 10 s. During the cyclic voltammetry curve (CV) analysis, a scanning rate of 10 mV/s was applied within the potential range of −0.8 to 0.6 V [26]. In the case of the chemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) detection, a potential of 0.01 V was applied.

Element doping is an effective means to enhance the performance of carbon-based materials [27] and nitrogen from biomass can serve as an N source for in-situ doping [28]. In this study, NCQDs were synthesized using widely sourced available corn straw as a precursor (see Schematic diagram 1 insert in Fig. 1 ). This process significantly increased the yield of NCQDs to 30.5 ± 3.6 %, representing more than 75 % of the yield achieved by existing methods [29,30]. TEM images showed that the NCQDs exhibited an aggregated distribution (Fig. 1a ∼ b), with an average particle size of these NCQDs was 1.80 ± 0.24 nm (Fig. 1c ∼ d). Fast flourier transform (FFT) processing of HRTEM images further showed it was found that NCQDs exhibited typical graphite carbon (100) lattice stripe spacing of 0.20 nm (Fig. 1e ∼ h) [31].

Methane production was closely monitored during a batch fermentation process spanning 11 days (Fig. 2 a ∼ b). Remarkably, in a system without light exposure, HAs were found to exert a significant inhibitory effect on both cumulative CH4 yield and the maximum daily CH4 yield. Specifically, the cumulative and maximum daily CH4 yield of R(HA) decreased by 41.2 % and 52.2 %, respectively, when compared to R. After providing light, the cumulative CH4 yield of the HAs-inhibited system (R(HA)L) increased substantially to 223.0 ± 42.1 mL/g VS. Notably, there were two peaks in daily CH4 yield, observed on the 1st and 7th days, respectively. Moreover, after the 6th day, the cumulative CH4 yield of R(HA)L (159.0 ± 45.3 mL/g VS) exceeded that of R, but remained lower than RL. Ultimately, its cumulative CH4 yield was consistent with RL on the 11th day. These results indicated the role of light in promoting methanogenesis and its potential in alleviating HAs inhibition.

The introduction of NCQDs further amplified the cumulative CH4 yield to 293.7 ± 17.7 mL/g VS (R(HA)L NCQD) with a maximum daily CH4 yield reaching 178.0 ± 32.7 mL/g VS. Similar to R(HA)L, there was a second peak in daily CH4 yield on the 7th day. The enhanced methane production can be attributed to several factors, including the shielding effect of NCQDs on HAs, their light responsiveness, and their role in relieving HAs inhabitation on methanogens [12]. Furthermore, the photoexcitation of NCQDs leads to the generation of holes and electrons, which play crucial roles in this process. These free radicals facilitate the degradation of HAs and simultaneously provide electrons that support methanogenesis. As shown in Fig. 2c, when NCQDs (500 mg/L) and HAs (1.0 g/L) were irradiated in aqueous solution, •OH radicals were produced, measured at the concentrations of 4.5 × 10-6 moL/L and 3.5 × 10-6 moL/L, respectively. Importantly, it is worth noting that such concentrations of •OH radicals did not have adverse effects on microbial mortality [36].

Fulvic acid, a vital component of HAs, is soluble in water [37]. Therefore, the changes of DOM content and components in the digester are correlated with HAs. sCOD serves as a representative indicator for measuring the total content of DOM. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, the HAs inhibition system, represented by R(HA), exhibited a relatively high residual sCOD of 1250 ± 76.3 mg/L, mainly due to decreased methanogenesis levels. Upon exposure to illumination, the sCOD content in the digestate decreased, with the most significant reduction observed in digesters containing NCQDs, where the sCOD content decreased to 450 ± 58.2 mg/L. This lower sCOD content can be attributed to the higher levels of methanogenesis, indicating a reduction in HAs inhibition levels.

The 3D-EEM spectrum revealed the changes in the composition of DOM in the digestate after AD (Fig. 3b ∼ f). The fluorescence spectrum was divided into five regions based on the emission and excitation wavelength [38,39]. Regions I and IV mainly consist of easily biodegradable substances, such as proteins (tyrosine) and soluble microbial byproducts within the DOM. Regions II, III, and V represented proteins (tryptophan), fulvic acid-like, and humic acid-like organic compounds [39]. As depicted in Fig. 3b and c, in the absence of HAs inhibition, DOM was mainly distributed across regions II-V and displayed consistent peaks (Em/Ex = 414/314 and Em/Ex = 416/318). The peak intensity located in region V significantly decreased (Fig. 3c). These results indicate that the AD process did not lead to the formation of new HAs under light conditions, potentially contributing to the observed increase in CH4 yield in the AD system with light exposure.

In the HAs inhibition system (Fig. 3d ∼ f), DOM was predominantly distributed in regions III-V, indicating a higher degree of humification. In R(HA), a high-intensity peak was observed at Em/Ex = 424/344, signifying a higher content of humic acid-like components in the system. With the introduction of light and NCQDs, the peak intensity gradually decreased and shifted toward region III. Fig. 3f revealed that DOM in R(HA)L NCQD exhibited two weak peaks at Em/Ex = 435/345 and Em/Ex = 440/227 in the V, indicating a decrease in humic acid-like content and substantial compositional changes.

To gain further insights into the changes of HAs components in different digesters, DOM was analyzed using ESI FT-ICR MS [40]. Fig. S4 a displays the mass spectrum of DOM in an HAs solution with the same concentration as in the AD system (1.0 g/L). Notably, there were high-intensity peaks at standard masses m/z (mass-to-charge ratio) 256.8 and 369.2. After AD, the highest peak in terms of m/z experienced changes and its intensity decreased (Fig. S4 b ∼ d). In the system with illumination, the peak at m/z 256.8 exhibited a significant decrease in the intensity. Conversely, the peak intensities increased at m/z 327.1 and 407.2. These findings indicated that the structure of HAs underwent alternations during AD, especially in the photo-AD system (R(HA)L and R(HA)L NCQD). Fig. S5 provides close-up views at nominal m/z 255 of DOM in different systems. The characteristic peak intensity of DOM C11H1O7 in HAs significantly decreased after AD. When exposed to light, the hydrogen content of the high-strength components in R(HA)L and R(HA)L NCQD decreased, indicating that these components undergoing oxidation.

Van Krevelen diagram displayed the variation in the seven components of DOM. As shown in Fig. 4a, the main DOM of HAs was cellulose-like substances, which was consistent with the composition of HAs. AD significantly reduced the content of cellulose-like components in the system compared to the DOM components of HAs (Fig. 4b ∼ d). The percentage of 7 components was quantitatively determined based on the proportion of relative intensity ( Table S4 ). In R(HA)L NCQD, the relative proportion of lignin substances in DOM were the lowest at 29.70 %. Compared to the DOM of HAs, this component decreased by 28.9 %. Both R(HA) and R(HA)L showed a similar degree of decrease. Additionally, the percentage of substances with lower molecular weights such as proteins, carbohydrates, and tannins has increased.

Double bond equivalents (DBE) represents the degree of unsaturation of the carbon skeleton, and its value is related to the complexity of the substance's structure [41]. Fig. 5 a shows that more high carbon number (24 ∼ 33) and high DBE (15 ∼ 22) components appeared in the AD system, which can be attributed to the degradation of HAs. DBE-O6 is a more representative parameter indicating unsaturation. The DOM of HAs possessed a high content of low carbon number and low DBE-O6 components (with high relative peak intensity) in Fig. 5b. In the late stage of AD, the high relative content components of DOM (DBE-O6) were mainly characterized by high carbon numbers (Fig. 5c). In R(HA)L and R(HA)L NCQD, there were almost only two components, C18/(DBE-O6)10 and C18/(DBE-O6)11 (Fig. 5d and e).

The results above indicated that light and NCQDs promoted the degradation of HAs into micromolecules in the AD system, which have the potential to be further utilized by microorganisms as carbon sources for methanogenesis. Fig. 5f provide a schematic diagram of this process.

The content and composition of EPS play a crucial role in microbial functions, including aggregation and information transmission [42,43]. Microorganisms often adjust their secretion levels and types to adapt to different environments, leading to variations in EPS composition. Notably, it was observed that exposure to light led to an increase in EPS content in Fig. S6 a. In addition, when microorganisms faced inhibition from HAs, they produced more EPS, likely as a self-protective response to counteract the effects of HAs [44]. In a digester inhibited by HAs, exposure to light further elevated the EPS content, particularly in the presence of NCQDs. Specifically, the EPS-TB and EPS-LB reached TOC concentrations of 128.9 ± 11.2 and 244.0 ± 27.6 mg/L, respectively. Compared with R(HA), this represented an 89.7 % increase in the content of EPS-TB. Interestingly, while the polysaccharide content in EPS remained relatively consistent across all experimental groups, ranging from 3.14 to 3.61 mg/g (Fig. S6 b), the protein content experienced significant changes.

The content of EPS-TB was only 0.02 ± 0.01 g/L in R (HA). However, under conditions of light exposure and the combined effects of NCQDs with light, EPS-TB content increased dramatically, rising by 16.5-fold and 17.5-fold, respectively. It is worth noting that during the AD process, microorganisms transfer electrons through direct interspecies electron transfer (DIET) [45]. Research has shown that the carrier of this transfer pathway involves microbial nanowires composed of functional proteins [46,47]. Therefore, the increase of protein content in EPS-TB is likely to not only enhance the aggregation of microorganisms but also improve the level of DIET. The changes in EPS content and composition identified in this study are among the key factors contributing to the promotion of increased CH4 yield in HAs-inhibited photo-AD systems by NCQDs.

The electrons generated by photocatalytic semiconductors serve as a potential source for methane synthesis by microorganisms. Surprisingly, it was unexpected to note that HAs exhibited a complete CV curve and I-t variation curve similar to NCQDs (Fig. 6a and b), indicating their ability to respond to light. The response wavelength range of HAs was mainly concentrated in the visible light range above 400 nm (Fig. 6c). Fig. 6d shows that the substrates in R(HA) and R(HA)L NCQD generated transient photocurrent. This suggests that the increase in cumulative CH4 yield in R(HA)L can be attributed not only to the photo-induced degradation of HAs and their DOM, but also to the utilization of photoelectrons generated from HAs by microorganisms, further enhancing methanogenesis. However, the substrates obtained in R(HA)L did not generate photocurrents, precisely indicating that the structure of HAs underwent changes and lost their photo-responsive characteristics after 11 days of irradiation. On the other hand, the substrates in R(HA)L NCQD exhibited higher photocurrents compared to R(HA)L, especially within the first 140 s. These results suggest that the combination of NCQDs and HAs generated more photoelectrons utilized by microorganisms. Furthermore, substrates containing NCQDs exhibited lower electrochemical impedance, resulting in a more efficient photocurrent transfer to microorganisms (Fig. 6e). The electron transfer occurring at the biotic-abiotic interface between photo-responsive material and microorganisms was critical in promoting methanogenesis. A schematic diagram of this conversion mechanism is provided in Fig. 6f.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microbial community structure and functional genes
ce:section.ce:para: Based on 16 s rRNA, the community structure of each digester was identified using high-throughput sequencing. The clustering analysis results (Fig. 7 a and b) indicate there were 23 shared OTUs among the bacterial communities and only 1 shared OTU among the archaeal communities, highlighting significant alterations in microbial community structures in response to lighting and other environmental factors. In R(HA)L NCQD, the number of unique OTU in both bacteria and archaea decreased due to an increase in the abundance of dominant species. As shown in Fig. 7c and d, the relative abundance of the dominant bacteria group, Bacilli, in R(HA)L NCQD reached 83.2 %. Compared with R(HA), this represents a 77.6 % increase in Bacilli relative abundance. Methanosarcinia was the dominant archaeal group in R(HA)L NCQD, with a relative abundance of 83.1 %, indicating a 38.5 % increase compared to R(HA). The upregulation of Bacilli and Methanosarcinia enhanced hydrolysis and methanogenesis levels during the AD process.

Additionally, there were significant changes in the content of coenzyme F420 (Fig. 7e). The content of coenzyme F420 in RL increased by 54.9 % compared to R. In the HAs inhibition system, the content of coenzyme F420 in R(HA)L NCQD was 1.47 ± 0.07 μmoL/L, representing an approximately 8-fold increase compared to R(HA). According to previous analysis results, the decrease in HAs inhibition level and the driving force of light on methanogenesis were potential reasons leading to an increase in the abundance of dominant species and enzyme content. In addition, the ATP content was found to be correlated with light intensity. However, the increase in ATP content cannot be solely attributed to the supply of photoelectrons, as ATP content was also higher in RL. At this point, microorganisms can still produce more ATP by converting organic substrates at high activity levels. However, it can be confirmed that exposure to light and the addition of NCQDs have a positive impact on microbial metabolism.

In order to further reveal the intrinsic mechanism of photo-driven methanogenesis and the conversion pathway of photoelectrons at the biotic-abiotic interface, qPCR was used to quantitatively analyze the content of representative hydrogenase and reductase involved in the methanogenesis. The percentage content of the enzymes was calculated based on the gene copynumber, as shown in Fig. 7f. [NiFe]-hydrogenase (Ech) is an important enzyme involved in the anaerobic respiration of methanogens [48], playing a pivotal role in electron reception and transfer. In R (HA)L and R(HA)L NCQD, the percentage of subunit EchA was relatively high, reaching ∼ 28 % and ∼ 40 %, respectively. This observation aligns with our earlier analysis, which suggests that Ech is involved in receiving and transferring the photoelectrons generated by HAs and the combined action of NCQDs with HAs at the interface (Fig. 8 ). Metanophenazine-dependent hydrogenase (Vht) is one of the electronic carriers used in the DIET process, contributing to the formation of microbial nanowires for electron conduction [49]. Simultaneously, Heterodisulfide reductase (Hdr) is an enzyme found in methanogens that participates in the reduction of CoM-S-CoB coenzyme, thereby generating the energy required for methanogens (as shown in Fig. 8) [50]. Moreover, Hdr plays a role involved in the reduction of archaeal flavin-based electron carriers during anaerobic respiratory processes. However, the proportion of average copynumber of VhtA and HdrD in R(HA)L NCQD was reduced compared to RL and R(HA), accounting for 55 % and 4 %, respectively. The predominance of EchA and diminished representation of VhtA and HdrD indicated that in an environment where photocatalysis generated photoelectrons, photo-driven methanogenesis exhibited a more pronounced potential for promotion than methanogenesis reliant on microbial electron transfer.

The formation of HAs during AD is an irreversible process, and its accumulation beyond the threshold will inevitably exert an inhibitory effect on microorganisms. The traditional methods for mitigating this inhibition mainly rely on “blocking” mechanisms. However, this study takes into account the unique properties of HA and introduces a novel strategy termed “elimination and promotion”. This approach harnesses light as the driving force for specially designed NCQDs to alleviate the inhibitory effect of HAs. In this innovative approach, NCQDs first bind to HAs within the HAs-inhibited AD system, and the combination was stimulated by light to produce oxidized species and photoelectrons. The main oxidizing species was •OH, which degraded the HAs through oxidation, thereby reducing the degree of humification. The resulting smaller micromolecule substances formed after degradation hold the potential to serve as microbial substrates, supporting microbial growth and metabolism. In addition, the study reveals that NCQDs, either alone or in combination with HAs, form a biotic-abiotic interface with microorganisms, creating an artificial photosynthetic system where the electron acceptor enzymes capture photoelectrons and transfer them to the methanogenic microorganism for methanogenesis. Overall, this research offers a comprehensive understanding of the dual role of semiconductors in photo-AD systems, presenting new avenues for the development of novel AD reactors and accelerators.

Qiang Liu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Haoyu Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Investigation, Data curation. Shuo Chen: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Investigation. Yeqing Li: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Mahmoud Mazarji: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization. Lu Feng: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision. Junting Pan: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Hongjun Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Funding acquisition. Chunming Xu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization.

Nat. Microbiol.

Chem. Eng. J.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrogen is a preferred intermediate in the energy-conserving electron transport chain of Methanosarcina barkeri
Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci
